fragments.content: Psychosis refers to a group of symptoms involving loss of contact with reality, including delusions (fixed false beliefs despite contrary evidence), hallucinations (sensory perceptions without external stimulus, auditory most common), disorganized thinking/speech (loose associations, word salad, tangentiality), and grossly disorganized/abnormal motor behavior (including catatonia). Psychosis occurs in schizophrenia spectrum disorders, mood disorders with psychotic features (bipolar, depression), substance-induced psychosis, medical conditions (delirium, dementia, neurological disorders), and brief psychotic disorder. First-episode psychosis typically emerges in late adolescence/early adulthood. Differential diagnosis critical as treatment varies by etiology.

First-episode psychosis: early intervention programs improve outcomes. Antipsychotic medication first-line—start low, go slow to minimize side effects; second-generation (aripiprazole, risperidone, olanzapine) often preferred initially due to lower EPS risk, though metabolic monitoring essential. Address comorbid substance use. Psychosocial interventions: CBT for psychosis, family psychoeducation, supported employment/education, case management. Duration of untreated psychosis correlates with poorer outcomes—rapid assessment/treatment critical. For mood disorders with psychotic features: antipsychotic + mood stabilizer/antidepressant. Medical/substance-induced: treat underlying cause. Clozapine for treatment-resistant cases. Long-term maintenance reduces relapse risk.

Psychosis is a symptom cluster, not a standalone DSM-5 diagnosis. Assessment determines underlying etiology: Schizophrenia (≥6 months with functional decline), Schizophreniform (1-6 months), Brief Psychotic Disorder (<1 month, full return to baseline), Delusional Disorder (non-bizarre delusions ≥1 month, preserved functioning), Schizoaffective (psychosis + mood episode with ≥2 weeks psychosis without mood symptoms), Mood Disorder with Psychotic Features (psychosis only during mood episodes), Substance/Medication-Induced (temporal relationship to substance/medication), Psychosis Due to Medical Condition (evidence from history/exam/labs). Rule out delirium (fluctuating consciousness, inattention). Comprehensive workup includes labs, toxicology, neuroimaging when indicated.
